reasoning: Robinhood ended Q1 2026 (March) at 27.4M funded customers and disclosed 27.6M in April and 27.7M in May 2026, i.e. net adds decelerating to roughly +110-190K/month, so a single further month of June growth points to ~27.85M at quarter-end. Growth is now mostly organic (the TradePMR and Bitstamp step-ups are already in the base), and Trump Accounts launched July 4, after the quarter closed, so it cannot lift the Q2 print. My median sits ~130K below the market's implied 27.99M, which appears to extrapolate an accelerating add rate that the April-to-May slowdown does not support.
evidence: Q1 2026 (Mar 31) actual: funded customers 27.4M, +1.7M or 6% YoY; investment accounts 29.1M (Q1 2026 earnings release, 2026-04-28)
April 2026 monthly operating data: 27.6M funded customers
May 2026 monthly operating data (released 2026-06-09): 27.7M funded customers, ~+110K MoM, +1.76M YoY (~6.8%)
Net adds trend implies ~+100-150K in June; ~300K total added March-to-May
June 2026 full operating data is not released separately — it comes with the Q2 print (~2026-08-28), so no confirming datapoint exists yet
Trump Accounts initiative launched 2026-07-04, i.e. after quarter-end, so its acquisition impact falls in Q3 not Q2

reasoning: Hims reported 2584k subscribers in Q1 2026 (+9% YoY, +73k QoQ), following a 2025 cadence of roughly +30k to +75k net adds per quarter as the compounded-GLP-1 unwind capped growth. Q2 2026 revenue guidance of $680-700M (+25-28% YoY) and the branded Wegovy/GLP-1 pivot imply a genuine acceleration in net adds, so I model ~+120k QoQ to ~2705k. That sits below the market's implied 2776k, which would require ~+192k net adds — a level Hims has not hit since early 2024 — so I see the consensus as modestly too aggressive, though my p90 covers it.
evidence: Q1 2026 actual: 2584k subscribers vs 2366k in Q1 2025 (+9% YoY), per company press release
Prior quarters: Q2 2025 = 2439k, Q4 2025 = 2511k — net adds of only ~30-75k per quarter through 2025
Q2 2026 revenue guidance $680-700M (+25-28% YoY) vs Q1 2026 revenue of $608M (+4% YoY), signaling sharp reacceleration
FY2026 guidance raised to $2.8-3.0B revenue; management says growth 'accelerates from here' with 2030 target of $6.5B+ revenue

No explicit subscriber guidance is given by the company, so the metric is inferred from revenue trajectory and the branded GLP-1 mix shift (higher ARPU per sub, so revenue growth overstates sub growth)

reasoning: eBay guided Q2 2026 GMV to $21.3-21.7B (8-10% FX-neutral), but management has beaten GMV guidance materially in each of the last several quarters (Q4 2025: 8% FXN actual vs 4-6% guided; Q1 2026: 14% FXN vs a similar conservative guide), and a weak dollar has added ~3-4pts of as-reported tailwind. Against a $19.5B year-ago base, a typical 2-4pt beat plus sustained FX benefit lands around $22.1-22.2B, though the explicitly flagged reversion of gold/silver bullion volumes and the lapping of prior-year marketing efficiencies are real decelerants. I land essentially at the market's ~$22.20B, a touch lower, because I put somewhat more weight on the bullion headwind actually biting.
evidence: Q2 2026 company guidance: GMV $21.3-21.7B, 8-10% organic FX-neutral growth (issued with Q1 2026 results, late April 2026)
Q1 2026 actual GMV $22.197B, +18% as-reported / +14% FX-neutral - a large beat vs guidance
Year-ago comparable: Q2 2025 GMV $19.5B (+6% as-reported, +4% FX-neutral)
Q4 2025 GMV $21.2B, +10% as-reported / +8% FXN vs guidance of 4-6% - establishes a pattern of conservative guidance
Management explicitly flagged Q2 deceleration from lapping prior-year marketing efficiencies and gold/silver bullion volumes reverting to historical levels
FX added ~4pts to Q1 2026 as-reported growth (~$78M to revenue), a tailwind likely to persist into Q2

reasoning: Starbucks ended FY2025 at 40990 stores and has added only 139 net new in H1 FY2026 (Q1 +128 to 41118; Q2 +11 to 41129, dragged by 62 'Back to Starbucks' closures incl. 48 in China), so hitting the reaffirmed 600-650 net-new FY2026 guidance would require ~480-510 in a back-half-loaded H2. Openings should accelerate (Q4 is seasonally the biggest quarter, closures largely complete, ~650 gross openings planned with ~500 international), but the H1 run-rate makes the top of guidance unlikely. I sit ~100 below the market's 41586 because I weight the actual H1 shortfall more heavily than management's unchanged full-year store target.
evidence: FY2025 year-end (Sep 28, 2025) total global stores: 40990; company-operated 21514
FY2026 guidance reaffirmed at Q2: ~600-650 net new coffeehouses globally (company-operated + licensed)
Q2 FY2026 included 62 closures from restructuring (48 in China); prior-year Q2 FY2025 comparison was 40789
US 16944 and China 7991 stores = 61% of portfolio; growth mix now skews international/licensed

reasoning: FY2025 actual was 2997829 passengers carried on 24.43M capacity days; NCLH's latest 2026 guidance is ~26.25M capacity days (+7.4%) at ~104.2% occupancy, implying ~27.3M passenger cruise days (+8.2%). Passenger count is growing faster than cruise days because average itinerary length is shrinking sharply with the Caribbean/short-cruise capacity build (Q1 2026: passengers +28.7% vs PCD +14.6%, avg length 7.71 vs 8.65 days); applying a more modest full-year length decline (~8.43 to ~8.0 days) gives ~3.4M. I sit slightly above the market's 3362987 because I weight the itinerary-mix shortening more heavily, though the mid-year guidance cut on Middle East disruption and softer demand caps the upside.
evidence: FY2025 reported: 2997829 passengers carried, 25278352 passenger cruise days, 24433624 capacity days, 103.5% occupancy
FY2026 guidance (Q1 update): ~26.25M capacity days, ~104.2% occupancy (cut from 105.7% at Q4 due to Middle East disruption/softer demand)
Capacity growth ~7% in 2026 from new deliveries (Norwegian Aqua/Luminara/Oceania Allura class additions) plus private-island short-itinerary deployment
Implied average cruise length falling from 8.43 days (2025) toward ~8.0, mechanically boosting headcount growth above cruise-day growth

reasoning: H1 2026 is locked at 838149 (Q1 358023 + Q2 480126), so the forecast reduces to H2, where Tesla laps a pull-forward-inflated Q3 2025 (497099) but a weak post-credit Q4 2025 (418227). Q2's 480k was flattered by drawing ~30k from the Q1 inventory build (production only ~450k), so I anchor H2 near a 470-490k/quarter run-rate as production ramps into a seasonally strong Q4, giving ~955k and ~1.79M for the year. I sit above the market's 1740196 and well above the stale sell-side 1.65M consensus, which was set before the 74k Q2 beat and has not been marked up; my downside case is a production-capped, demand-softening H2 rather than a return to decline.
evidence: Q2 2026 actual: 480126 deliveries, +25% YoY, ~74k above the 406024 analyst consensus; first YoY growth since 2023
Q1 2026 actual: 358023 deliveries vs 365645 consensus, with production 408386 (~50k inventory build)
2025 actuals: Q3 497099, Q4 418227, FY ~1636000 (-8%, second straight annual decline)
Sell-side FY2026 consensus 1654808 (median 1667842) published pre-Q2 and cut from 1689691 in March — not yet updated for the Q2 beat
Q2 2026 production (~450k) below deliveries (480k) implies inventory drawdown and caps near-term upside; Musk's 20-30% growth talk remains the bull case

reasoning: FY2025 GMV was ~$79.6B (Q1 18.8 + Q2 19.5 + Q3 20.1 + Q4 21.2); 2026 is already tracking well above trend with Q1 actual $22.2B (+18% as-reported, +14% FX-neutral) and Q2 guided to $21.3-21.7B (+8-10% FXN, ~100bp FX tailwind). Bottom-up, holding H2 as-reported growth at ~7-9% (deceleration as bullion volumes normalize and marketing-efficiency comps get harder) gives Q3 ~$21.7B and Q4 ~$22.9B, for FY2026 ~$88.3B. I sit slightly above the market's $87.97B because the reported figure is as-reported dollars and the current FX tailwind plus collectibles/motors momentum skews the distribution modestly to the upside, though eBay's own 7-7.5% FXN full-year guide argues for real downside if H2 decelerates sharply.
evidence: Q1 2026 actual GMV $22.2B, +18% as-reported / +14% FX-neutral (reported 2026-04-29); focus categories collectibles, motors, fashion grew 24% combined
Q2 2026 guidance $21.3-21.7B, 8-10% FX-neutral growth with ~100bp FX tailwind; revenue guided $2.97-3.03B
Management raised FY2026 FX-neutral GMV growth guidance to 7-7.5% (from 6.5-7.5%) — conservative vs. H1 run-rate, implying assumed H2 deceleration as gold/silver bullion volumes revert
Active buyers +1% to 136M; growth is being driven by spend per enthusiast buyer (>$3400/yr) and AI/live-shopping initiatives rather than buyer count, a mild cap on upside

reasoning: Meta ended 2025 at 78865 and Q1 2026 at 77986, then began cutting ~8000 roles (~10%) on May 20 while cancelling 6000 open requisitions, which mechanically puts run-rate headcount near 70000 by mid-year. Management flagged further, unquantified H2 2026 reductions, partially offset by continued aggressive AI/infrastructure hiring and the 7000 internal redeployments, so I see only modest net rebuild in H2. I sit ~2000 below the 72722 implied central estimate because the market appears to underweight the announced second-half cuts and the 6000 cancelled reqs that cap re-hiring.

Altria's 2025 reported domestic cigarette shipments were ~61.8B units (-10.0%), but declines have moderated for four straight quarters: Q1 2026 reported volume was 13867M units, just -2.4% YoY (-4% adjusted for trade inventory, vs -12% adjusted in Q1 2025), as illicit flavored e-vapor saturation and FDA enforcement slowed cross-category migration. Offsetting that, Marlboro continues to lose ~1.4 share points YoY, Q1's reported figure was flattered by an inventory build that should reverse, and the FDA's May 2026 enforcement-discretion policy could put 100-200 new vapor products on shelves in H2, re-accelerating declines. I center on a ~5% full-year reported decline (~58.6B), modestly above the market's implied ~58.14B (-5.9%) because the moderating trend is running better than that through Q1, with a wide left tail for an H2 re-acceleration.
evidence: FY2025 reported domestic cigarette shipment volume ~61.8B units, -10.0% vs 2024 (~68.7B); adjusted -9.5%, industry -8%
Q1 2026: 13867M units vs 14204M in Q1 2025, -2.4% reported / ~-4% adjusted; industry -5% vs -9% prior year
Fourth consecutive quarter of sequential improvement in industry decline rate, attributed to illicit e-vapor saturation plus enforcement
Marlboro total category retail share 39.7% in Q1 2026, down 1.4 points YoY — ongoing share loss adds to industry decline
Altria reaffirmed FY2026 guidance with EPS weighted to H2; guidance assumes limited impact from illicit-vape enforcement
FDA May 2026 enforcement-discretion policy may add 100-200 vapor/pouch products to shelves in H2 2026, a downside risk to cigarette volumes

reasoning: Meta reported 77986 at 3/31/26, then notified ~8000 employees (~10%) starting 5/20/26 while freezing 6000 open roles. Meta's disclosure precedent (Nov 2022 cuts still 'substantially all' included in the 12/31/22 count; 2023 cuts only fully removed a quarter or two later) implies most of the May cohort is still on the 6/30/26 reported headcount, with only APAC/fast-exit and attrition reducing it. I therefore sit above the market's 74500, expecting the bulk of the drop to land in Q3 2026, though my p10 covers the scenario where Meta removes the cohort immediately.

May 20, 2026: ~8000 layoffs (~10% of workforce) begun, plus 6000 open roles cancelled and ~7000 staff redeployed to AI teams; Zuckerberg ruled out further broad cuts

Precedent: Meta's reported headcount has historically included notified-but-not-yet-exited employees (Nov 2022 layoffs still counted at 12/31/22; Q3 2023 was the first period a 'substantial majority' of impacted staff were excluded)
Only ~6 weeks elapsed between notification and the 6/30/26 measurement date, shorter than the 2022 analog
Hiring offset is minimal given the 6000-role freeze, though Meta continues aggressive AI-researcher hiring

reasoning: Q1 2026 ended at 8167000 Active CLEAR+ Members (+13.0% Y/Y, +551000 sequential), but that quarter was flattered by a TSA-shutdown-driven acquisition surge plus retention gains, so Q2 net adds should normalize well below 551k; historical Q1-to-Q2 seasonality is the weakest sequential step (Q2 2025 was -2000 vs Q1 2025). Offsetting that, momentum is genuinely strong — Q2 revenue guidance of $268-271M (+22.8%) and Q1 bookings +40.8% imply continued healthy gross adds and improved churn from eGates/free-family features. I land at roughly +218k net adds (8.385M, ~16% Y/Y), modestly above the market's implied +176k because bookings acceleration and retention improvements look under-weighted, though I keep a wide left tail for shutdown-cohort pull-forward.

reasoning: CAVA ended Q1 FY2026 with 459 CAVA restaurants (+20 net new) and raised FY2026 guidance to 75-77 net new units, leaving 55-57 for Q2-Q4; applying CAVA's historical back-half-weighted cadence (Q2 took ~28% of post-Q1 openings in 2025) implies ~16-18 net new in Q2, or ~475-477. I sit marginally above the market's 476 because the pipeline is running ahead of plan (guidance was already raised once), new-market entries in the Midwest are accelerating, and the later-than-usual ~Sept 10 report date hints at a longer fiscal Q2 than the 12-week 2025 comparable, which skews the upper tail. Downside risk is modest and mostly construction/permitting slippage plus occasional closures, so the distribution is tight and slightly right-skewed.

Q2 2025 base was 16066 million cigarettes, and Altria's reported decline rate has moderated sharply each quarter: -10.2% (Q2'25), -8.2% (Q3'25), -7.9% (Q4'25), -2.4% (Q1'26, or ~-4% adjusted for a trade-inventory build). I expect underlying declines near -4% with a partial reversal of Q1's inventory tailwind, giving a reported decline of roughly -4% to -5.5%. My median (~-4.8%) sits modestly above the market's implied -5.4%, since I weight the improving trend (illicit e-vapor saturation plus enforcement) a bit more heavily.
evidence: Q2 2025 actual cigarette shipment volume: 16066 million units (Marlboro 14458M), down 10.2% y/y
Q1 2026: reported domestic cigarette shipment volume -2.4% to 13867 million units; -4% adjusted for trade inventory movements, vs -12% adjusted in Q1 2025
Sequential moderation in reported declines: Q3 2025 -8.2%, Q4 2025 -7.9%, Q1 2026 -2.4%
Altria attributes moderation to slowing cross-category migration as illicit flavored e-vapor saturates and enforcement increases; company reaffirmed FY2026 guidance in April
Q1 2026 benefited from a trade-inventory build (~1.6pp), which typically reverses and would make the Q2 reported figure weaker than the underlying rate

reasoning: Q1 2026 ended at 2.584M subscribers (+9% YoY, only +73K QoQ off the 2.511M 2025 exit), so reaching ~3.06M would require net adds to more than double to ~160K/quarter for three straight quarters. The raised $2.8-3.0B FY26 revenue guide does imply a large H2 ramp, but a meaningful share of that comes from ARPU expansion as branded GLP-1s (Novo partnership, Wegovy-class pricing) replace the ~$80/month blended rate, which lowers the subscriber count needed to hit the revenue target. I therefore sit slightly below the market at ~2.95M, with wide bands because gross adds of 100K+/month in weight loss could overwhelm high GLP-1 churn to the upside, or the pivot could stall net growth to the downside.
evidence: Q1 2026 actual: 2.584M subscribers, +9% YoY; monthly revenue per average subscriber fell to $80 from $85
FY2025 exit: 2.511M subscribers, +13% YoY vs 2.229M at end of 2024 (only ~282K net adds in all of 2025)
FY2026 guidance raised to $2.8-3.0B revenue (+19-28%) with Q2 guide $680-700M; management targets 100000+ monthly weight-loss subscriber adds (gross) via the branded GLP-1/Novo Nordisk pivot
Q1 2026 revenue grew only 4% YoY with US revenue -8%; growth is skewed to H2 and to international (+969% YoY) plus higher-priced branded products, i.e. ARPU as much as subscriber count

reasoning: Q1 2026 items sold is already reported at 721.7M (+47% YoY, an acceleration from Q4 2025's +43%), leaving ~2.69B to come from Q2-Q4 against 2025 bases of ~522M/635M/752M. Assuming the free-shipping/1P-driven volume push decelerates gradually to roughly +44%/+40%/+35% gives ~3.38B, and I sit slightly above that (~+42% FY growth) because MELI has beaten on item volumes for five straight quarters and unit growth is currently accelerating, not fading. I am ~1% above the market's 3.368B, so my main differentiation is distribution width rather than center: the downside tail reflects harder H2 comps and Argentina FX/macro risk.

FY2026 quarterly build: 721.7 + ~752 + ~889 + ~1015 = ~3.38B baseline, adjusted up for persistent beat pattern

reasoning: Q1 2026 posted 933M orders (+27% Y/Y); applying the typical Q1->Q2 sequential lift (2025: 732M->761M, +4.0%; and Q4'25->Q1'26 +3.3%) gives ~965-970M, and management's Q2 Marketplace GOV guide of $32.4-33.4B implies ~+4% QoQ GOV with AOV still rising slightly faster than order count. Deliveroo remains an inorganic tailwind (closed Oct 2025, not yet lapped), keeping Y/Y growth near 27%. I sit ~4M below the market's 973M because DoorDash missed the order consensus in Q1 (933M vs 954M expected), suggesting sell-side order models are running modestly hot.

Seasonal Q1->Q2 order step-up historically +2.4% to +4.0%

reasoning: Alphabet's Q1 2026 supplemental disclosure reported 194668 employees (vs 185719 in Q1 2025), a +4.8% YoY and a large +3848 sequential jump from 190820 at year-end 2025, signaling a re-acceleration in hiring tied to AI and Cloud that management explicitly said would continue. Applying a sequential add of roughly +2000 to +3000 — larger than the +1384 Q1-to-Q2 add in 2025 given the stronger current run-rate, but tempered by reported 2026 cloud/security layoffs of ~1500-3000 — puts my median near 197100, about 1000 above the market's ~196129, which I think under-weights the Q1 hiring surge.

Q2 2025 actual was 187103 (Q1 2025 to Q2 2025 delta of +1384)
Q1 2026 call: R&D expense +26% driven by compensation for AI talent; company expects to continue hiring in AI and Cloud
Offsetting: 2026 press reports of Google layoffs of roughly 1500-3000 in cloud/cybersecurity/threat-intel teams

reasoning: Reddit's DAUq series runs 108.1M (Q1'25), 110.4M (Q2'25), 116.0M (Q3'25), 121.4M (Q4'25), 126.8M (Q1'26), i.e. sequential adds of +5.4M/quarter for three straight quarters but a much weaker +2.3M in Q2'25 due to summer seasonality plus Google algorithm volatility in logged-out traffic. Applying a normal ~+5M trend add discounted for that recurring Q2 seasonal drag gives ~130M (+18% YoY on an easy compare), with growth now led by international (+26% YoY) while US DAUq is nearly flat at +7%. I sit slightly below the market's 131.14M because the implied +3.4% QoQ ignores how consistently Q2 underperforms trend, though wide tails reflect logged-out DAU's dependence on Google referral swings.

Mix shift: international DAUq +26% YoY vs US +7%, so growth is increasingly driven by non-US logged-out/translated content, which is more sensitive to search-referral volatility
Q2 2026 is the final quarter Reddit discloses the logged-in/logged-out DAUq split, adding modest definitional/reporting uncertainty

reasoning: Schwab's disclosed monthly series runs 38730K (Jan-26), 39099K (Mar-26/Q1), 39300K (Apr), 39536K (May), i.e. net monthly adds accelerating from ~185K to ~236K on record gross openings (461K in May, +37% YoY). Extrapolating one more month of ~240K net adds puts June 30, 2026 active brokerage accounts near 39.78M, about 30K above the market's 39749342 implied central estimate. Downside tail reflects periodic account-purge/attrition true-ups that occasionally break the trend; upside tail reflects continued acceleration in gross openings.

metric: Employees (Full-Time And Part-Time; Excludes Contractors & Temporary Personnel)
reasoning: Amazon reported 1576000 employees at YE2025 (vs 1556000 at YE2024) and held roughly flat at ~1575000 in Q1 2026 despite the 30000 corporate cuts announced since Oct 2025, showing operations hiring is offsetting corporate shrinkage. The corporate reductions hit only ~350k of a ~1.2M-strong ops base, and Q4 fulfillment build historically lifts the Dec 31 figure, so I expect flat-to-slightly-up rather than the modest decline implied by the market. I sit ~11k above the market's 1569366 because Q1 2026 actuals already absorbed most of the announced layoffs without a net drop.

reasoning: Ford's H1 2026 U.S. sales were 1006515 (Q1 457315 + Q2 549200), down ~9.6% from H1 2025's 1113386, hurt by the Novelis aluminum shortfall on F-Series, a 69% cut in daily-rental fleet, and the Escape/Lincoln Corsair phaseouts. H2 2025 was 1090738 and was itself aluminum-constrained in Q4, so the comp is easier and Ford says supply normalizes in H2 2026, implying a milder H2 decline of roughly 3-7% and a full year near 2.04-2.05M. I sit modestly above the market's 2025862 because the second-half F-Series recovery plus Ford's 16.0-16.5M SAAR assumption more than offsets the structural fleet/model-exit drag.

Alphabet's officially reported headcount was 190820 at Dec 31 2025 and 194668 at Mar 31 2026 (+4.8% YoY, +3848 in Q1 alone), with hiring reaccelerating alongside the raised $180-190B 2026 capex plan and AI talent build-out. Applying the recent pattern where Q1 carries an outsized share of annual adds (2025: +2396 of +7497) to a hotter 2026 base implies roughly +6000-8000 more over Q2-Q4, or ~5-6% full-year growth. I land ~1700 below the market's 203290 because Alphabet has consistently paired AI investment with headcount discipline (voluntary exits, flat 2024, periodic role eliminations), so I weight a decelerating back half more than the market appears to.
evidence: Q1 2026 8-K supplemental: Number of employees 194668 (Mar 31 2026) vs 185719 (Mar 31 2025), +4.8% YoY
FY2024 was nearly flat (+0.45%) and FY2023 fell 4.1% after layoffs — recent history shows headcount can stall abruptly
Q1 2026 results: revenue +22% to $109.9B, Cloud +63%, 2026 capex guidance raised to $180-190B with 2027 to 'significantly increase' — supports continued hiring
Third-party workforce trackers (Revelio) show ~24700 active Alphabet job postings in 2026, ~98% above prior year, indicating accelerated hiring velocity

reasoning: Ferrari shipped 13640 units in 2025 (-1% y/y) and management explicitly designed volumes to be substantially flat to ease a major model change-over that continues through 2026, so unit growth is deliberately suppressed while revenue growth (~€7.5B guided) comes from mix and personalization. Q1 2026 shipments were 3436, down 4.4% y/y, which annualizes toward ~13050-13500, though H2 typically catches up with new model ramps (including the Elettrica) and Ferrari manages the full-year number tightly. I sit slightly below the market's 13662 because the only 2026 hard data point so far is a 4.4% decline and management has shown no appetite for volume growth during the changeover.

Items sold have accelerated for four straight quarters (Q2'25 +31%, Q3'25 +39%, Q4'25 +43%, Q1'26 +47% to 721.7M), driven by Brazil's lower free-shipping threshold, and Q2'25 (550.1M, +31%) is still a soft comp since the Brazil inflection only showed up from Q3'25. Applying the stable Q1->Q2 sequential step of roughly +12-13% to 721.7M implies ~810-815M, or ~+48% YoY, so I sit modestly above the market's 804.7M (which embeds slight deceleration to ~+46%). Risks skew to slower unit growth if Argentina/Mexico normalize or FX-driven mix shifts toward higher-ticket items, hence a wider downside tail.
evidence: Q1 2026 actual: 721.7M items sold, +47% YoY (Brazil +56%, Mexico +37%, Argentina +35%)
Year-ago Q2 2025 actual: 550.1M items sold, +31% YoY - a comp set before the Brazil free-shipping acceleration
Trend of accelerating YoY growth: 31% -> 39% (Q3'25, 635.2M) -> 43% (Q4'25, 752M) -> 47% (Q1'26)

reasoning: Active CLEAR+ Members were 8167K at 3/31/26 (+13.0% YoY) versus 7616K at 12/31/25 and 7187K at 12/31/24 — a Q1'26 net add of ~551K vs just ~42K in Q1'25, a sharp structural acceleration corroborated by Q1'26 Total Bookings +40.8% YoY and revenue +19.7%. Assuming adds moderate from that pace to roughly 200-300K per quarter through Q2-Q4'26 (Q4 is seasonally weakest), year-end 2026 lands near 8.95M, ~17.5% YoY. I sit above the market's ~8.76M because that implies only ~580K of adds across the remaining three quarters, which understates the demonstrated Q1 run-rate and the bookings-to-member lead indicator.

reasoning: FY2025 payers averaged ~13.8M (-5% Y/Y) and Q1 2026 came in at 13.5M (-5% Y/Y) with RPP +10%, so the monetization-over-volume strategy continues to shrink the payer base. Applying the normal seasonal step-down (2025 ran ~14.2/14.1/13.9/13.5) to a 13.5M Q1 base, with modest moderation from Tinder's returning registration growth and Hinge's expansion, yields an FY2026 average near 13.1-13.2M (about -4.5% Y/Y). I sit slightly above the market's ~12.97M because that implies a re-acceleration of decline to ~-6% despite Q1 tracking at -5% and management's stabilization signals; downside tail allows for continued Tinder weakness or brand divestitures.
evidence: Q1 2026: payers 13.5M, -5% Y/Y; RPP $20.90, +10%; revenue $864M (+4% reported, flat FX-neutral)

CEO Rascoff cites March 2026 new-user registrations returning to Y/Y growth and improving Tinder ecosystem indicators, arguing against a steepening decline

reasoning: Grab reports the annual MTU figure as the average across the year (FY2025 = 47.2M avg vs ~50M Q4 exit; FY2024 = 41.3M), so FY2026 should average the four 2026 quarters rather than the exit run-rate. Q1 2026 printed 52M (+17% YoY), and extrapolating the recent ~1.5-2M sequential quarterly adds gives roughly 52/54/55.5/57 = ~55M average. I sit ~2M below the market's 57.09M implied central estimate, which I think mistakes the Q4 exit level for the full-year average.
evidence: FY2025 Group MTUs 47.2M, +14% from 41.3M in FY2024 (Grab Q4/FY2025 earnings release)
Grab exited 2025 'crossing 50 million' MTUs — exit level ~3M above the reported annual average, confirming averaging convention

FY2026 guidance reaffirmed at $4.04-4.10B revenue / $700-720M adj. EBITDA — steady, not accelerating, top-line trajectory

reasoning: Reported total customers ran 711 (Q4'24), 769, 849, 911, 954, 1007 (Q1'26), i.e. sequential adds of +58, +80, +62, +43, +53 — averaging ~59 with no upward trend despite accelerating revenue, since growth is now driven by expansion within large accounts rather than logo count. Applying a ~50-60 add to the 1007 base gives a median near 1063, marginally below the market's 1068 because I weight the decelerating four-quarter add trend more than the strong Q2'25 (+80) seasonal comp. Upside tail allows for a bootcamp/AIP-driven surge back toward +85 adds.
evidence: Q1 2026 (Mar 31, 2026) reported 1007 total customers vs 769 a year earlier (~31% y/y), per the FY2026 Q1 10-Q/press release

Sequential adds: Q1'25 +58, Q2'25 +80, Q3'25 +62, Q4'25 +43, Q1'26 +53 — mean ~59, flat-to-decelerating
Revenue growth accelerated to 85% y/y in Q1 2026 driven by expansion (US commercial 615 customers, +42% y/y), not logo count, so customer count is a lagging/slower series
Palantir does not guide to customer count, so the figure is trend-extrapolation rather than guidance-anchored

reasoning: NCLH guided Q2 2026 to ~6.6M capacity days at ~102.5% occupancy, implying ~6.77M passenger cruise days; the swing factor is average cruise length, which has been falling fast as short Caribbean/Great Stirrup Cay itineraries and Prima-class ships mix in (8.53 days in Q2 2024, 8.51 in Q2 2025, 8.10 in Q4 2025, 7.71 in Q1 2026). Applying a 6-9% YoY length decline to Q2 2025's 8.51 days (tempered because Q2 is Europe-heavy, where the short-itinerary shift is weaker) gives ~7.8-7.9 days and ~860k passengers. I sit ~4% above the market's 825577, which implies only a ~3% length decline and looks like it under-extrapolates the Q4 2025/Q1 2026 mix shift; downside risk is occupancy missing guidance given the demand-driven FY26 EPS cut.
evidence: Q1 2026 actual: 861060 passengers carried vs 669099 in Q1 2025 (+28.7%) on capacity days +12.1% and occupancy 103.8% vs 101.5% - implied avg cruise length fell from 8.65 to 7.71 days
Q2 2025 actual (year-ago comp): 738635 passengers, 6052273 capacity days, 103.9% occupancy (~8.51 day avg length); Q2 2024: 711918 passengers
Company Q2 2026 guidance: ~6.6 million capacity days and ~102.5% occupancy; FY2026 ~26.25M capacity days at ~104.2%
Q4 2025: 786827 passengers on 6263064 capacity days at 101.8% (~8.10 days) vs 8.83 days in Q4 2024 - confirms the length-compression trend predates Q1 2026

NCLH cut FY2026 adjusted EPS guidance to $1.45-1.79 from $2.38 on fuel costs and softer European demand, and said it entered 2026 behind its booking curve - a modest downside risk to the 102.5% occupancy assumption
NCLH has recently beaten its own occupancy guidance (Q3 2025 came in at 106.4% vs ~105.5% guided), supporting slight upside skew on the PCD base

reasoning: Coinbase reported $202B total trading volume in Q1'26 (down from $296B in Q4'25 and $393B in Q1'25), and industry CEX spot volume fell a further 27.9% QoQ in Q2'26 with Bitcoin -14.2% and a May volume trough. A naive pass-through of the industry decline implies ~$146B, but Coinbase hit all-time-high crypto market share in Q1 and is growing derivatives, prediction markets and DEX-routed volume, so I land at ~$158.5B — modestly above Kalshi's ~$156.7B and below FactSet consensus ($168.5B) and Oppenheimer ($162B). Skew is slightly right because institutional block/derivatives volume is the lumpy component and June already showed a rebound.
evidence: Q1'26 actual total trading volume $202B ($36B consumer / $166B institutional), vs Q4'25 $296B and Q1'25 $393B — two consecutive down quarters

Sell-side: FactSet consensus ~$168.5B; Oppenheimer cut its Q2'26 estimate 13% to $162B; Baird sees Q2 revenue 5-6% below consensus
Kalshi implied ~$156.7B with ~41% odds above $160B and ~99% above $150B
Offsetting positives: record Q1 crypto trading volume market share, retail derivatives >$200M annualized revenue, record prediction-market and DEX-enabled volumes

reasoning: Carnival's Q2 FY2026 release (June 2026) guides full-year 2026 ALBDs to 97.4 million (+1.0% vs FY2025's ~96.5M), with 48.4M already booked in H1 actuals and 24.9M guided for Q3, implying ~24.1M in Q4. ALBDs are a near-mechanical capacity metric — fixed fleet, scheduled dry docks, only two small deliveries — so with two-thirds of the year already realized the residual uncertainty is well under 1%. I sit just above the market's 97,351,587 because Carnival's mid-year ALBD guidance has historically been met or slightly exceeded (fewer unplanned dry-dock days), though I keep the distribution tight and only mildly skewed."], "key_evidence": ["Q2 FY2026 earnings release: full-year 2026 ALBD guidance of 97.4 million, capacity growth ~1.0%

Boeing delivered 314 jets in H1 2026 (+12% y/y, best first half since 2018) versus 600 for full-year 2025, and the FAA cleared 737 MAX rate 47 in late May with a 53/month target by year-end, so H2 should run meaningfully ahead of H1 as it has in every recent year. Bottom-up, CFO Malave's ~500 737 guidance plus a widebody run-rate annualizing near 200 (787 ramp, 767, 777F/777X) points to roughly 690-715, while a typical H2/H1 seasonal ratio of ~1.15-1.20 on 314 gives ~675-690. I sit above the market's 651 because that implies H2 essentially flat with H1 despite an approved rate increase; my downside tail allows for the usual Boeing ramp slippage, supplier/fuselage constraints, or a quality/labor disruption.
evidence: FY2025 actual 600 deliveries (447 737, 88 787, 35 777, 30 767), highest since 2018
FAA cleared 737 MAX rate 47/month in May 2026; Boeing targeting 53/month exiting 2026
CFO Jay Malave guided to ~500 737-family deliveries for FY2026
Historical H2>H1 seasonality (H1 2025 ~280 vs H2 2025 ~320) argues against the market's flat-H2 implied 651

reasoning: Q1 2026 shipments fell 23.5% to 155M cans purely on trade-inventory movements while Nielsen offtake still grew ~10%, so the shipment/consumption gap should partially unwind against an unloaded Q2 2025 base of 190M cans. The June 2026 US launch of ZYN ULTRA adds a pipeline-fill tailwind that isn't in the flat-YoY market estimate, partially offset by real share loss to Velo Plus (Zyn +1pt vs Velo +6pts in Circana 52-week data). I sit ~3% above the market's 190.8M, with a wide band because PMI shipment timing has swung ±20% versus offtake in recent quarters.
evidence: Q1 2026 actual: 155M cans, -23.5% YoY, attributed to distributor/trade inventory movements in both current and prior-year periods; offtake +10%

PMI FY2026 guidance: high-single-digit smoke-free shipment volume growth; no explicit ZYN can guidance, capacity ~900M+ with new Colorado plant (300M annualized by end-2026) and Kentucky expansion
ZYN ULTRA launched in the US in June 2026 (9mg/11mg, 20-count moist format) as a response to Velo Plus, implying Q2 trade loading
Competitive erosion: Circana 52-weeks to June 14, 2026 shows Zyn share +~1pt vs Velo Plus +~6pts; Zyn US pouch volume share ~61.5% at end-2025
